revision.text:
[[arz:ايكى]][[az:İki adası]][[be:Ікі]][[ceb:Ikino Shima]][[ckb:دوورگەی ئیکی]][[cs:Iki (ostrov)]][[de:Iki (Insel)]][[en:Iki Island]][[es:Isla Iki]][[et:Iki saar]][[eu:Iki uhartea]][[fa:ایکی‌نوشیما]][[fr:Île Iki]][[ja:壱岐島]][[ko:이키섬]][[lt:Ikis (sala)]][[pl:Iki (wyspa)]][[pt:Ilha Iki]][[ro:Insula Iki]][[ru:Ики (остров)]][[sv:Ikiöarna]][[tr:Iki Adası]][[uk:Ікі (острів)]][[zh-yue:壹岐島]][[zh:壹岐島]] {{Short description|Archipelago in the Tsushima Strait}}{{More citations needed|date=May 2023}} {{Expert attention|Volcanoes | date = May 2023|Geology }} {{Infobox islands | name = Iki | native_name = 壱岐島, ''Iki-no-shima'', ''Iki rettō'' | sobriquet = | image_name = Iki-no-shima map.jpg | image_size = | image_caption = Map of Iki-no-shima | image_alt = | map_image = Iki in Nagasaki Prefecture Ja.svg | map_caption = Map of Nagasaki Prefecture with Iki Islands in red | pushpin_map = Japan#Japan Nagasaki Prefecture | pushpin_relief = 1 | pushpin_map_caption = Location of Iki Islands | location = | coordinates = {{coord|33|47|N|129|43|E|type:adm1st_scale:3000000|display=inline,title}} | archipelago = | waterbody = {{ill|Tsushima Strait|en|Tsushima Strait|qid=Q52055|lt=Tsushima Strait|1=Tsushima Strait}} | total_islands = 4 inhabited, 18 total | major_islands = | area_km2 = 138.46 | area_footnotes = | Rank = | length_mi = | length_footnotes = | width_mi = | width_footnotes = | coastline_mi = | coastline_footnotes = | elevation_m = 212.9 | elevation_footnotes = | highest_mount = Takenotsuji | country = {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | country_admin_divisions_title = {{ill|Prefectures of Japan|en|Prefectures of Japan|qid=Q50337|lt=Prefectures|1=Prefectures of Japan}} | country_admin_divisions = Nagasaki | country_admin_divisions_title_1 = {{ill|Cities of Japan|en|Cities of Japan|qid=Q494721|lt=City|1=Cities of Japan}} | country_admin_divisions_1 = {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|en|Iki, Nagasaki|qid=Q861519|lt=Iki|1=Iki, Nagasaki}} | demonym = | population = 28,008 | population_as_of = 2013 | population_footnotes = | density_km2 = 202 | density_footnotes = | ethnic_groups = {{ill|Japanese people|en|Japanese people|qid=Q161652|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese people}} | website = | additional_info = }} [[File:Saruiwa of Iki island 2007.JPG|right|250px|thumb|Saruiwa of Iki Island]] {{nihongo|'''Iki Island'''|壱岐島|Iki-no-shima}}, or the {{nihongo|'''Iki Archipelago'''|壱岐諸島|Iki-shotō|}}, is an archipelago in the {{ill|Tsushima Strait|en|Tsushima Strait|qid=Q52055|lt=Tsushima Strait|1=Tsushima Strait}},{{Cite web |title=Iki {{!}} island, Japan {{!}} Britannica |url=https://www.britannica.com/place/Iki |access-date=2023-05-09 |website=www.britannica.com |language=en}} which is administered as the city of {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|en|Iki, Nagasaki|qid=Q861519|lt=Iki|1=Iki, Nagasaki}} in {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|qid=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|1=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The islands have a total area of {{convert|138.46|sqkm|sqmi}} with a total population of 28,008. Only four (4) of the twenty-three (23) named islands are permanently inhabited. Together with the neighboring islands of [[Tsushima Island|Tsushima]], they are collectively within the borders of the {{ill|Iki-Tsushima Quasi-National Park|en|Iki-Tsushima Quasi-National Park|qid=Q1072765|lt=Iki–Tsushima Quasi-National Park|1=Iki-Tsushima Quasi-National Park}}.{{Cite web |title=Iki-Tsushima Quasi-National Park Travel Guide: Tourist Attractions & Things to Do |url=https://trek.zone/en/japan/places/25602/iki-tsushima-quasi-national-park |access-date=2023-05-10 |website=Trek Zone |language=en}} ==Geology== The Iki Islands are volcanic in origin: they are the exposed and eroded {{ill|Basalt|en|Basalt|qid=Q43338|lt=Basalt|1=Basalt}}ic summit of a massive {{ill|Quaternary|en|Quaternary|qid=Q26185|lt=Quaternary|1=Quaternary}} {{ill|Stratovolcano|en|Stratovolcano|qid=Q169358|lt=Stratovolcano|1=Stratovolcano}} last active over 600,000 years ago.{{Cite web |date=2021-06-25 |title=Global Volcanism Program {{!}} Iki Volcanic Group |url=https://volcano.si.edu/volcano.cfm?vn=282819 |access-date=2023-05-09 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210625023013/https://volcano.si.edu/volcano.cfm?vn=282819 |archive-date=2021-06-25 }}{{Verification needed|date=May 2023|reason=Seems this is in it, but I am not knowledgeable enough about volcanoes to verify this}} Iki Island is slightly oval in shape, and measures approximately {{convert|17|km|mi}} from north-south and {{convert|14|km|mi}} from east-west. The highest elevation is {{nihongo|Takenotsuji|岳ノ辻|}}, a weakly curved peak with a highest elevation at {{convert|212.9|m|ft}} above sea level. The average height of the land surface is 100 meters above sea level.National Geospatial Intelligence Agency (NGIA). ''Prostar Sailing Directions 2005 Japan Enroute''. Prostar Publications (2005). {{ISBN|1577856511}}{{Verification needed|date=May 2023}} The archipelago is approximately {{convert|20|km|nmi}} north-northeast of the [[Kyushu]] coast at its closest point and southeast of the Tsushima Islands.{{Verification needed|date=May 2023}} ==History== The Iki Islands have been inhabited since the {{ill|Japanese Paleolithic|en|Japanese Paleolithic|qid=Q844172|lt=Japanese Paleolithic|1=Japanese Paleolithic}} era, and numerous artifacts from the [[Jōmon period|Jōmon]], {{ill|Yayoi period|en|Yayoi period|qid=Q207858|lt=Yayoi|1=Yayoi period}} and [[Kofun period]]s have been found by archaeologists, indicating continuous human occupation and activity.{{Cite web |last=Fans |first=Japan |date=2021-07-19 |title=Iki Islands |url=https://japanfans.nl/en/iki-islands/ |access-date=2023-05-10 |website=Japan Fans}} In the [[Kojiki]] Iki island is one of the islands [[Izanagi]] and {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} gave birth to. It is the fifthborn.{{cite book |last=Chamberlain |first=Basil Hall |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=zVw392cJrJoC |title=The Kojiki: Japanese Records of Ancient Matters |publisher=Forgotten Books |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-60506-938-8 |pages=75 |access-date=9 February 2011}} ==={{Anchor|Ikikoku}}Ikikoku=== [[File:Wajinden diagram.svg|thumb|Map illustrating the path from the {{ill|Daifang Commandery|en|Daifang Commandery|qid=Q704994|lt=Daifeng commandery|1=Daifang Commandery}} to {{ill|Yamatai|en|Yamatai|qid=Q844295|lt=Yamatai|1=Yamatai}}, and its distances in the {{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}}.]] In the Chinese ''Wèizhì Wōrén chuán'' (Japanese 魏志倭人伝, ''Gishi {{ill|Wajinden|en|Wajinden|qid=Q1135798|lt=Wajinden|1=Wajinden}}''), part of the ''{{ill|Records of the Three Kingdoms|en|Records of the Three Kingdoms|qid=Q247318|lt=Records of the Three Kingdoms|1=Records of the Three Kingdoms}}'' dating from the third century, mention is made of a country called “Iki”, (一支国, Iki-koku), located on an archipelago east of the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}}. Archaeologists have tentatively identified this with the large Yayoi period settlement of {{ill|Harunotsuji Site|en|Harunotsuji Site|qid=Q11409340|lt=Harunotsuji|1=Harunotsuji Site}}, one of the largest to have been discovered in Japan, where artifacts uncovered indicate a close contact with the Japanese islands and the Asian mainland. It is sometimes referred to as "{{Nihongo|Idaikoku|一大国}}", which may be a typographic error.{{Cite web |last=世界大百科事典内言及 |title=一大（支）国とは |url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E4%B8%80%E5%A4%A7%28%E6%94%AF%29%E5%9B%BD-1269426 |access-date=2022-07-07 |website=コトバンク |language=ja}} In historical records. It was said to have bigger fields than {{ill|Tsukaikoku|ja|対海国|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukaikoku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Tsukaikoku|qid=Q11458165}} and a population three times larger. There is a museum dedicated to the country located in {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|qid=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki|1=Nagasaki}}.{{Cite web |title=Iki City Ikikoku Museum |url=https://www.discover-nagasaki.com/en/sightseeing/100050 |access-date=2023-05-10 |website=【公式】長崎観光/旅行ポータルサイト ながさき旅ネット |language=en}} ===Iki Province=== {{Main|Iki Province}} The islands were organized as {{ill|Iki Province|en|Iki Province|qid=Q1052938|lt=Iki Province|1=Iki Province}} under the [[Ritsuryō]] reforms in the latter half of the seventh century, and the name ''Iki-no-kuni'' appears on wooden markers found in the imperial capital of {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}.{{Cite web |title=Historical and geographical dictionary of Japan {{!}} WorldCat.org |url=https://www.worldcat.org/title/77691250 |access-date=2023-05-09 |website=www.worldcat.org |language=en}} During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, the island was attacked by {{ill|Jurchen people|en|Jurchen people|qid=Q753880|lt=Jurchen|1=Jurchen people}} pirates in the {{ill|Toi invasion|en|Toi invasion|qid=Q703655|lt=Toi invasion|1=Toi invasion}} of 1019. Afterwards, the islands came under the rule of the {{ill|Matsura clan|en|Matsura clan|qid=Q11531840|lt=Matsura clan|1=Matsura clan}}, who developed trade and commercial relations between {{ill|Goryeo|en|Goryeo|qid=Q28208|lt=Goryeo|1=Goryeo}} in Korea, Tsushima, Iki and Kyushu. However, the islands were again devastated by the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} {{ill|Mongol invasions of Japan|en|Mongol invasions of Japan|qid=Q208156|lt=Mongol invasions of Japan|1=Mongol invasions of Japan}} in 1274 and 1281 during which time many of its inhabitants were slaughtered. Throughout the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the islands were a main base for the Japanese {{ill|Wokou|en|Wokou|qid=Q697605|lt=Wokou|1=Wokou}} pirates, who plundered coastal settlements in Korea and China. Following the establishment of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the islands came under the rule of {{ill|Hirado Domain|en|Hirado Domain|qid=Q3034453|lt=Hirado Domain|1=Hirado Domain}}. ===Modern period=== Following the [[Meiji Restoration]], the islands became part of "Hirado Prefecture" from 1871, which then became part of Nagasaki Prefecture. The islands were fortified with numerous {{ill|Coastal artillery|en|Coastal artillery|qid=Q1358324|lt=Coastal artillery|1=Coastal artillery}} batteries during {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}, but did not experience any combat. Remains of these fortifications can be found on the island of Wakamiyajima, north of the main Iki Island. On November 18, 1948, Lt. William Downham, from the USAF 36th Fighter Group stationed at [[:en:Ashiya Air Field|Ashiya Air Field]], experienced engine failure in his {{ill|North American P-51 Mustang|en|North American P-51 Mustang|qid=Q192075|lt=North American P-51 Mustang|1=North American P-51 Mustang}} while patrolling the {{ill|Korea Strait|en|Korea Strait|qid=Q52052|lt=Korea Strait|1=Korea Strait}} between Japan and the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}}. He successfully parachuted onto Iki Island.http://www.aviationarchaeology.com, http://www.accident-report.com/USAF.html {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190302032605/http://www.accident-report.com/usaf.html |date=2019-03-02 }} In the 1960s and 1970s, in particularly the town of {{ill|Iki, Nagasaki|en|Iki, Nagasaki|qid=Q861519|lt=Iki|1=Iki, Nagasaki}}, the islanders were notorious for overfishing, but blamed declining catches on the local species of whales and dolphins. In 1977, the local fishermen invited television companies to film the mass slaughter of dolphins. In response, activists heavily condemned the fishermen's acts of killing the dolphins.{{Cite web |title=Greenpeace - Iki Island Dolphin Massacre |url=https://media.greenpeace.org/archive/Iki-Island-Dolphin-Massacre-27MZIF25NQVO.html |access-date=2023-05-09 |website=media.greenpeace.org}} In view of the already endangered {{ill|Japanese amberjack|en|Japanese amberjack|qid=Q1202973|lt=Japanese amberjack|1=Japanese amberjack}}, the local town government banned large-scale, commercial fishing of Japanese amberjack after 1982. A group of small scale fishermen in 2016 were reported as fighting back against this overfishing.{{Cite web |title=In Japan, a David vs Goliath Battle to Preserve Bluefin Tuna |url=https://e360.yale.edu/features/in_japan_a_david_versus_goliath_battle_to_preserve_bluefin_tuna |access-date=2023-05-09 |website=Yale E360 |language=en-US}} As of 2018 fishermen are protesting against large vessels from {{ill|Mainland Japan|en|Mainland Japan|qid=Q4113790|lt=Mainland Japan|1=Mainland Japan}} overfishing around Iki island.{{Cite magazine |last1=Yagishita |first1=Yuta |date=2020-10-01 |title=No More Tuna for Japan's Sushi? |url=https://www.thenation.com/article/archive/no-more-tuna-for-japans-sushi/ |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201001113159/https://www.thenation.com/article/archive/no-more-tuna-for-japans-sushi/ |archive-date=2020-10-01 |access-date=2023-05-09 |magazine=The Nation}} ==In popular culture== The island features in the 2020 video game ''{{ill|Ghost of Tsushima|en|Ghost of Tsushima|qid=Q42564798|lt=Ghost of Tsushima|1=Ghost of Tsushima}}''.{{Cite web |last=Downs |first=Patrick |date=2021-07-21 |title=Ghost of Tsushima: Journey into the dark secrets of Iki Island |url=https://blog.playstation.com/2021/07/21/ghost-of-tsushima-journey-into-the-dark-secrets-of-iki-island/ |access-date=2023-05-09 |website=PlayStation.Blog |language=en-US}} It is an expansion pack for the game with the main game taking place on [[Tsushima Island|Tsushima]].{{cite web |last=Ramsay |first=Robert |date=July 29, 2021 |title=Interview: Ghost of Tsushima: Director's Cut - Sucker Punch Talks PS5 Features, Iki Island Expansion, and Japanese Praise |url=https://www.pushsquare.com/news/2021/07/interview_ghost_of_tsushima_directors_cut_-_sucker_punch_talks_ps5_features_iki_island_expansion_and_japanese_praise |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20210926054353/https://www.pushsquare.com/news/2021/07/interview_ghost_of_tsushima_directors_cut_-_sucker_punch_talks_ps5_features_iki_island_expansion_and_japanese_praise |archive-date=September 26, 2021 |access-date=July 26, 2022 |work=[[Push Square]]}} ==Climate== {{Iki weatherbox}} ==Transportation== Iki Island has ferry terminals in Ashibe, Ishida and Gōnoura, which connect Iki to mainland Japan such as {{ill|Fukuoka|en|Fukuoka|qid=Q26600|lt=Fukuoka|1=Fukuoka}} and {{ill|Kitakyushu|en|Kitakyushu|qid=Q188806|lt=Kitakyushu|1=Kitakyushu}}. Located on the east coast {{ill|Iki Airport|en|Iki Airport|qid=Q1038664|lt=Iki Airport|1=Iki Airport}} connects the island to {{ill|Nagasaki Airport|en|Nagasaki Airport|qid=Q133796|lt=Nagasaki Airport|1=Nagasaki Airport}} in {{ill|Nagasaki|en|Nagasaki|qid=Q38234|lt=Nagasaki|1=Nagasaki}}. The {{ill|Japan National Route 382|en|Japan National Route 382|qid=Q333414|lt=Japan National Route 382|1=Japan National Route 382}} connects the hamlets of the island together, and the bus company "Iki-kōtsu" provides for public transport. ==Islands== {| class="wikitable sortable" ! class="unsortable" | Photo || Name || class="unsortable" | Kanji || Area
[km²] || Population || highest point
[m] || Peak || Coordinates |- |[[File:Position of Ashibe Port Iki City.png|50px]] | Iki Island | {{lang|ja|壱岐島}} | align="right" | 133.8 | align="right" | 13,178 | align="right" | 213 | Takenotsuji | {{Coord|33|47|N|129|43|E|display=inline|type:isle|name=Iki-no-shima}} |- | [[File:Harushima Iki.png|50px]] | Harushima | {{lang|ja|原島}} | align="right" | 0.53 | align="right" | 140 | align="right" | | | {{Coord|33|43|23|N|129|38|56|E|display=inline|type:isle|name=Harushima}} |- | [[File:Nagashima Iki.png|50px]] | Nagashima | {{lang|ja|長島}} | align="right" | 0.51 | align="right" | 170 | align="right" | | | {{Coord|33|43|38|N|129|37|53|E|display=inline|type:isle|name= Nagashima}} |- | [[File:Oshima Iki.png|50px]] | Oshima | {{lang|ja|大島}} | align="right" | 1.16 | align="right" | 200 | align="right" | | | {{Coord|33|44|17|N|129|38|5|E|display=inline|type:isle|name=Oshima}} |- | [[File:Wakamiyajima Iki.png|50px]] | Wakamiyajima | {{lang|ja|若宮島}} | align="right" | 0.35 | align="right" | - | align="right" | 99.7 | | {{Coord|33|51|56|N|129|41|11|E|display=inline|type:isle|name=Wakamiyajima}} |- |- class="sortbottom" | colspan="8" | {{GeoGroup|article= Iki Island }} |} ==References== {{Wikivoyage|Iki}} {{Commons Category|Iki Island}} {{reflist}} [[en:Iki Island]] [[arz:ايكى]] [[az:İki adası]] [[be:Ікі]] [[ceb:Ikino Shima]] [[ckb:دوورگەی ئیکی]] [[cs:Iki (ostrov)]] [[de:Iki (Insel)]] [[es:Isla Iki]] [[et:Iki saar]] [[eu:Iki uhartea]] [[fa:ایکی‌نوشیما]] [[fr:Île Iki]] [[ja:壱岐島]] [[ko:이키섬]] [[lt:Ikis (sala)]] [[pl:Iki (wyspa)]] [[pt:Ilha Iki]] [[ro:Insula Iki]] [[ru:Ики (остров)]] [[sv:Ikiöarna]] [[tr:Iki Adası]] [[uk:Ікі (острів)]] [[zh:壹岐島]] [[zh-yue:壹岐島]] {{wikidata link|Q494914}} [[Category:Archipelagoes of Japan]] [[Category:Archipelagoes of the Pacific Ocean]] [[Category:Iki Island]] [[Category:Islands of Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Landforms of Nagasaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages linked to by wikidata]] [[Category:Pleistocene stratovolcanoes]] [[Category:Stratovolcanoes of Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
